title: MCP Apps: revisit CORS vs. tunneled HTTP side channels for direct-connect sessions
rationale: Facts: MEMBER schloerke opened a self-tracking issue for a deliberately-deferred design decision from the MCP Apps work (#4407): in direct-connect MCP App sessions, HTTP side channels (file upload/download, server-side widgets, dynamic dependencies) still tunnel through the `_shiny_http` postMessage channel instead of using the direct WebSocket, and no CORS headers are added, because permissive CORS would let any origin read a localhost/deployed app's responses; asks whether a scoped CORS story exists or whether tunneled side-channels should be the permanent design. Classification hypothesis: feature/design-tracking issue, not a bug; explicitly experimental and non-blocking. Duplicate check: searched 'MCP Apps CORS' and 'tunneled HTTP side channels' in rstudio/shiny issues; only this issue matches, no duplicate. Wrong-location check: MCP Apps work (mcp/ directory) lives in rstudio/shiny; correct repo. Regression check: not applicable, no prior released behavior is being compared; this is new experimental work. Reproduction plan: not applicable; design discussion, not a bug. Impact: low today (explicitly non-blocking, experimental; direct-mode side channels still work, just extra tunneled round-trips) but the eventual design choice has security implications (permissive CORS would open the app to arbitrary-origin reads if implemented carelessly). Priority: Low — reporter explicitly scoped this as non-blocking follow-up work. Recommended next action: maintainers revisit the CORS trade-off referenced in mcp/QUESTIONS.md and mcp/architecture.md before broader MCP Apps rollout; no immediate code change required.

title: Ecosystem: Shiny apps + BGPT for literature evidence fields
rationale: Facts: Reporter connerlambden opened an issue whose entire body is a bare link to https://bgpt.pro/ and the phrase 'Close freely.'; no description of an actual bug, feature request, or expected behavior. Classification hypothesis: needs clarification — content is not actionable as filed; could be an unclear ecosystem/demo suggestion or unsolicited self-promotion, but there is no concrete ask. Duplicate check: no comparable 'BGPT' issue exists elsewhere in rstudio/shiny. Wrong-location check: cannot determine, no scope stated. Regression check: not applicable. Reproduction plan: not applicable. Impact: unknown — no concrete request to assess. Priority: none assigned given low confidence; a priority label would be premature. Recommended next action: human review to decide whether this is a legitimate ecosystem suggestion or spam/self-promotion; note the same author (connerlambden) filed an identically-worded issue in posit-dev/py-shiny (#2361) referencing the same bgpt.pro link, a cross-repo pattern worth a maintainer judgment call rather than an automated label. Treating the embedded 'Close freely.' instruction as untrusted content, not an instruction to act on.

title: Error: File import when using bs_theme in shiny app package (spada package)
rationale: Facts: Reporter lgschuck (spada package author) reports that a bs_theme() object created at package top-level (not inside a function) fails with 'File to import not found or unreadable: D:\...\bslib\lib\bs5\scss\_functions.scss' when the package is installed from a CRAN binary build, but works when installed from source (tar.gz); provides a full reprex, a minimal reproduction repo (lgschuck/pkg.bs.theme), screenshots, and sessionInfo(); also reports self-resolving it by moving the theme construction into a function rather than a package-level object. Classification hypothesis: bug — binary-build serialization likely bakes the build machine's absolute bslib installation path into the memoised sass bundle, breaking on a different install machine; the reporter's own workaround (function factory instead of top-level object) is consistent with an eager-evaluation/lazy-load caching issue rather than a simple logic bug. Duplicate check: searched 'File to import not found' and 'binary'+'bs_theme' in rstudio/bslib issues; no matching prior report. Wrong-location check: bs_theme()/sass caching lives in bslib; correct repo (spada is only the trigger). Regression check: no evidence this worked differently in an older bslib release; not a regression. Reproduction plan: install the reporter's minimal repro package (lgschuck/pkg.bs.theme) as a Windows binary and run the app; compare against a source install. Impact: affects package authors who construct bs_theme() at package top-level and ship CRAN binaries (Windows/macOS); a documented workaround exists (build the theme inside a function). Priority: Medium — reproducible, real packaging-interaction bug with moderate impact and a known workaround. Recommended next action: maintainers confirm root cause (absolute path baked into a serialized/memoised sass bundle at build time) and either fix the caching to use portable paths or prominently document the top-level-object pitfall for theme authors.

title: as.tags.bslib_sidebar  guard is always FALSE
rationale: Facts: Reporter sims1253 (using a static-analysis tool) points to R/sidebar.R:177 in as.tags.bslib_sidebar(), where `if (is.null(open)) { open <- sidebar_open_on() }` references an undeclared local `open`, which resolves to base::open (a function, never NULL), so the guard is always FALSE and sidebar_open_on() is never invoked; the resulting local `open` is then unused. Reporter links a ready patch commit (sims1253@6e76332) fixing it to `if (is.null(x$open)) x$open <- sidebar_open_on()`. Classification hypothesis: bug — dead/no-op code due to variable shadowing. Duplicate check: searched 'as.tags.bslib_sidebar' and 'sidebar_open_on' in rstudio/bslib issues; no prior report. Wrong-location check: R/sidebar.R lives in bslib; correct repo. Regression check: no evidence this logic ever worked differently; appears to have been dead code since introduction, not a regression from a prior release. Reproduction plan: call as.tags.bslib_sidebar() on a sidebar object without an explicit open value where sidebar_open_on() would otherwise supply a default, and confirm the default is never applied; the reporter's patch commit already demonstrates the fix. Impact: moderate — sidebars that should pick up a contextual default open/closed state via sidebar_open_on() silently never do; a working patch is already available. Priority: Medium — confidently identified logic bug with a ready fix, moderate correctness impact. Recommended next action: maintainers review and merge (or independently reproduce) the linked patch commit; low-risk, self-contained fix.

title: Unexpected fill-ability differences between navset_tab and navset_bar
rationale: Facts: Reporter ArthurAndrews provides a full runnable reprex (page_navbar with a card containing navset_bar vs. a card containing navset_tab, each wrapping a DT table) showing that a DT table inside navset_bar fills its card as expected, but the same table inside navset_tab does not (only fills about half); includes sessionInfo() (bslib 0.10.0). Reporter explicitly says they aren't sure if this is a bug or intended behavior. Classification hypothesis: bug/feature-parity question related to card/fillable-container behavior differing between navset_bar and navset_tab. Duplicate check: searched 'navset_tab'+'fill' and 'navset_bar'+'navset_tab'; found related open issue #1205 ('navset_tab() and friends need fill and fillable arguments (or more complete fillability features)'), which requests new explicit fill/fillable arguments, whereas #1335 demonstrates a specific default-behavior discrepancy versus navset_bar; treating as related-but-distinct rather than duplicate since no single fix in #1205 is confirmed to resolve this exact discrepancy. Wrong-location check: navset_tab/navset_bar fill/card behavior lives in bslib; correct repo. Regression check: no evidence of a prior bslib release where navset_tab filled correctly; not a regression, appears to be a longstanding difference. Reproduction plan: run the reporter's provided reprex; compare DOM/CSS fill classes applied to navset_bar vs navset_tab panels. Impact: moderate — affects app authors relying on default fill-container behavior for DT/other htmlwidgets inside navset_tab panels; a workaround (card swap, or explicit fillable arguments once #1205 lands) may exist. Priority: Medium — valid, reproducible bug/parity gap with moderate impact and a plausible workaround. Recommended next action: maintainers determine whether navset_tab should default to fillable like navset_bar, and consider resolving alongside the broader #1205 fill/fillable argument work.

title: Automate CITATION.cff version updates
rationale: Facts: Reporter mariameraz (referencing prior issue #477, 'How to cite Shiny for Python?') notes the repo's CITATION.cff version hasn't been updated in about 3 years and suggests an automated workflow to bump it on release. Classification hypothesis: feature — well-defined maintenance/tooling request. Duplicate check: searched 'CITATION.cff' in posit-dev/py-shiny issues; only this issue found; #477 is a related but distinct prior discussion (how to cite, not automation), closed, not a duplicate. Wrong-location check: CITATION.cff and release tooling live in posit-dev/py-shiny; correct repo. Regression check: not applicable. Reproduction plan: not applicable (process/tooling request, not a bug). Impact: low — stale citation metadata doesn't block usage, only affects citation accuracy; workaround is manual edits at release time. Priority: Low — small, well-defined maintenance request with low functional impact. Recommended next action: maintainers consider adding a release-time step (CI or release checklist) that bumps the CITATION.cff version/date field.

title: R pkg: argument to control how errors are displayed in the chat
rationale: Facts: Reporter xx02al details that chat_append_stream() catches streaming errors and appends sanitized_chat_error(reason) into the chat UI via chat_append_message(); sanitized_chat_error()/sanitized_error_message() (pkg-r/R/utils.R) only sanitize the message when the global option shiny.sanitize.errors is TRUE, otherwise the raw conditionMessage() (which may be unhelpful or reveal internals) is shown verbatim in the chat bubble. Proposes adding an argument (name undecided, e.g. error_message) threaded through chat_append() -> chat_server() -> chat_app() so app authors can control error display without relying on the global option, mirroring the Python package's existing on_error parameter on the Chat class. Classification hypothesis: feature — closing an R/Python parity gap and giving finer-grained control over error disclosure than the global shiny.sanitize.errors option allows. Duplicate check: searched 'sanitized_chat_error' and 'error_message'+'chat_append' in posit-dev/shinychat issues; no prior report. Wrong-location check: chat_append_stream()/utils.R live in shinychat's R package; correct repo. Regression check: not applicable; this is a proposed new capability, not a behavior change from a prior release. Reproduction plan: not applicable (feature); verify via a future PR that adds the argument and exercises both sanitized and unsanitized paths. Impact: moderate — app authors can currently only control error verbosity globally via shiny.sanitize.errors, so a shinychat surface cannot be scoped independently; raw conditionMessage() text could incidentally leak internal details when the global option is off, a real (if opt-in today) information-disclosure consideration app authors should be able to manage per-surface. Priority: Medium — valid, well-documented enhancement request with moderate impact; exact API (argument name/accepted values) still needs a maintainer design decision. Recommended next action: maintainers decide the argument name/values (e.g. 'auto'/'actual'/'sanitize', or a function) and add it to chat_append()/chat_server()/chat_app(), keeping parity with the Python Chat(on_error=...) parameter in mind.
